en.extract:
The Ecuadorian tapaculo  or El Oro tapaculo  (Scytalopus robbinsi) is a small passerine bird belonging to the genus Scytalopus, a genus of tapaculos. It is restricted to a small area in south-western Ecuador and was not described until 1997.
It is a small tapaculo, 11 centimetres long. The bill is black and fairly heavy. The plumage is grey with a brown nape and rump and brown barring on the flanks. The tail is blackish. The female's underparts are browner than those of the male. The song is a series of double-notes repeated for about a minute.
The bird inhabits the undergrowth of humid forest between 700 and 1250 metres above sea-level on the Andean slope in El Oro Province, Guayas Province and Cañar Province and undoubtedly in the intervening Azuay Province. Its population is believed to be small and declining. It is threatened by the loss, degradation and fragmentation of its habitat and is classified as an endangered species by the IUCN. Part of its range lies within the protected Buenaventura Reserve.


References

 Data related to Scytalopus robbinsi at Wikispecies
BirdLife International (2007) Species factsheet: Scytalopus robbinsi.Downloaded from Archived 2007-07-10 at the Wayback Machine on 15/8/2007.

The '''Ecuadorian tapaculo''' <ref>Krabbe, N. & Schulenberg, T. S. 1997. Species limits and natural history of ''Scytalopus'' tapaculos (Rhinocryptidae), with descriptions of the Ecuadorian taxa, including three new species. Pp. 46-88 in Remsen, J. V. [Ed.] Studies in Neotropical ornithology honoring Ted Parker. Ornithological Monographs 48.</ref> or '''El Oro tapaculo''' <ref>Ridgely, R.S. & Greenfield, P.J. 2001 The Birds of Ecuador. Cornell University Press, Ithaca, NY</ref> ('''''Scytalopus robbinsi''''') is a small [[passerine]] [[bird]] belonging to the genus ''[[Scytalopus]]'', a genus of [[tapaculo]]s. It is restricted to a small area in south-western [[Ecuador]] and was not described until 1997.

It is a small tapaculo, 11 centimetres long. The [[beak|bill]] is black and fairly heavy. The [[plumage]] is grey with a brown nape and rump and brown barring on the flanks. The tail is blackish. The female's underparts are browner than those of the male. The [[bird song|song]] is a series of double-notes repeated for about a minute.<ref>{{Cite web | url=http://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Scytalopus-robbinsi | title=El Oro Tapaculo (Scytalopus robbinsi) :: Xeno-canto }}</ref>

The bird inhabits the undergrowth of humid [[forest]] between 700 and 1250 metres above sea-level on the Andean slope in [[El Oro Province]], [[Guayas Province]]<ref>{{Cite web | url=http://www.xeno-canto.org/192673 | title=XC192673 el Oro Tapaculo (Scytalopus robbinsi) }}</ref> and [[Cañar Province]]<ref>{{Cite web | url=http://www.xeno-canto.org/241054 | title=XC241054 el Oro Tapaculo (Scytalopus robbinsi) }}</ref> and undoubtedly in the intervening [[Azuay Province]].<ref>Krabbe, N. & Schulenberg, T. S. 1997. Species limits and natural history of ''Scytalopus'' tapaculos (Rhinocryptidae), with descriptions of the Ecuadorian taxa, including three new species. Pp. 46-88 in Remsen, J. V. [Ed.] Studies in Neotropical ornithology honoring Ted Parker. Ornithological Monographs 48.</ref> Its population is believed to be small and declining. It is threatened by the loss, degradation and fragmentation of its habitat and is classified as an [[endangered species]] by the [[IUCN]]. Part of its range lies within the protected Buenaventura Reserve.
